verses.text: A great cry rises: in the dearth the poor have mortgaged fields, borrowed for the king's tax, and even sold their children into bondage to their own brethren. Hardship is made worse by hard-hearted kin. Affliction within the family of God is a grief He will not ignore.

A great cry rises: in the dearth the poor have mortgaged fields, borrowed for the king's tax, and even sold their children into bondage to their own brethren. Hardship is made worse by hard-hearted kin. Affliction within the family of God is a grief He will not ignore.

A great cry rises: in the dearth the poor have mortgaged fields, borrowed for the king's tax, and even sold their children into bondage to their own brethren. Hardship is made worse by hard-hearted kin. Affliction within the family of God is a grief He will not ignore.

A great cry rises: in the dearth the poor have mortgaged fields, borrowed for the king's tax, and even sold their children into bondage to their own brethren. Hardship is made worse by hard-hearted kin. Affliction within the family of God is a grief He will not ignore.

A great cry rises: in the dearth the poor have mortgaged fields, borrowed for the king's tax, and even sold their children into bondage to their own brethren. Hardship is made worse by hard-hearted kin. Affliction within the family of God is a grief He will not ignore.

Nehemiah is angry and rebukes the nobles for charging interest to their brethren: 'we... redeemed our brethren; and will ye even sell them?' He confronts oppression head-on. Love that has bought back the captive will not stand by while the freed are enslaved again.

Nehemiah is angry and rebukes the nobles for charging interest to their brethren: 'we... redeemed our brethren; and will ye even sell them?' He confronts oppression head-on. Love that has bought back the captive will not stand by while the freed are enslaved again.

Nehemiah is angry and rebukes the nobles for charging interest to their brethren: 'we... redeemed our brethren; and will ye even sell them?' He confronts oppression head-on. Love that has bought back the captive will not stand by while the freed are enslaved again.

'It is not good that ye do,' he says; 'ought ye not to walk in the fear of our God?' and he bids them restore the lands and the usury. He sets their conduct under God's eye. The fear of God is the true cure for greed that wrongs a neighbour.

'It is not good that ye do,' he says; 'ought ye not to walk in the fear of our God?' and he bids them restore the lands and the usury. He sets their conduct under God's eye. The fear of God is the true cure for greed that wrongs a neighbour.

'It is not good that ye do,' he says; 'ought ye not to walk in the fear of our God?' and he bids them restore the lands and the usury. He sets their conduct under God's eye. The fear of God is the true cure for greed that wrongs a neighbour.

They promise to restore all; Nehemiah takes their oath and shakes out his lap, praying God so shake out everyone who breaks the word, and the people say 'Amen' and praise the LORD. The vow is sealed with solemn warning. Repentance proves itself by making restitution at once.

They promise to restore all; Nehemiah takes their oath and shakes out his lap, praying God so shake out everyone who breaks the word, and the people say 'Amen' and praise the LORD. The vow is sealed with solemn warning. Repentance proves itself by making restitution at once.

For twelve years as governor, Nehemiah did not take the food allowance his office allowed, though former governors had burdened the people. He spares those he leads. A godly leader uses his rights for others' good and lays them down rather than add to their load.

For twelve years as governor, Nehemiah did not take the food allowance his office allowed, though former governors had burdened the people. He spares those he leads. A godly leader uses his rights for others' good and lays them down rather than add to their load.

He kept building, fed many at his own table, and sought no land for himself, 'because of the fear of God'; 'think upon me, my God, for good.' His generosity flows from reverence, not ambition. The leader who serves in God's fear leaves his reward in God's hand.
